body:
Epay Policy is an option here.

Email from wickphillips below.

It would be a lift to get this setup. Perhaps Raquels team can do it.

I am Q Oliver, and I work with Matt Zucker here at Wick Phillips. Based on our review of ePayPolicy’s website and user reviews, ePayPolicy appears to be a legitimate and well-established payment processor. Notably, it is PCI Level 1 compliant, which is the highest standard for payment security. From a practical standpoint, integration with their platform and customization to meet Pinecrest’s operational needs seems straightforward. Plus, we’ve inquired to industry professionals in our network and were told that ePayPolicy is currently well regarded in the market.

Regarding compliance, ePayPolicy’s terms and conditions state that they ensure compliance with all applicable laws with regard to you (Pinecrest) and themselves regarding their services. However, below are the key considerations Pinecrest should monitor:

Notice & Consent Requirements - Pinecrest customers must:
Be provided notice of electronic business practices;
Have the option to opt out; and
Consent to conducting business online. This means customers must affirmatively consent to using ePayPolicy.

Prompt Payment of Claims & Premiums - Any portal used (including ePayPolicy) must:
Generate accessible receipts for clients;
Clearly document each transaction; and
Comply with Texas Department of Insurance rules regarding installment payment plans, including limits on down payments and number of installments.

Fee Disclosure - Under Chapter 4005 of the Texas Insurance Code, agents may charge fees for services, provided:
Fees are disclosed in writing before being charged;
Clients consent to the fees; and
Fees are itemized with a description of the services provided.
Based on ePayPolicy’s own disclosures, Pinecrest may pass through these fees to consumers, assuming proper documentation and consent are in place.

Please let us know if you’d like to schedule a call to walk through any of these items in more detail or if you have additional questions.

author: Aharon Heiman
body:
Claude note -

Did a solid sweep of the benefits-billing space. Before the list, one framing point that matters for how you choose, because "consolidated billing" actually spans a range:

At the light end, it's software you run yourself — you upload the carrier invoices, it merges them into one statement and flags discrepancies, but your team still does the work. At the heavy end, it's a full-service TPA (third-party administrator — a licensed company that handles money movement on your behalf) that becomes the single point of contact, collects one payment from the employer each month, pays each carrier, and in some cases even handles your commission payments. Where you land on that spectrum is the real decision. Given your book — 600+ mostly small DSP groups, voluntary/ancillary-heavy, W-2 workers, high turnover — the heavier full-service end tends to fit better, because high turnover means constant add/term churn that's painful to reconcile by hand.

Here are the five I'd put in front of you, roughly in order of fit:

1. Soluta — Probably the closest match to your situation. They're a licensed TPA in all 50 states, which lets them actually collect premiums, pay carriers on behalf of groups, and handle broker commission payments. They run 60+ carrier integrations and have billed/collected for around 6,000 groups and 1.3 million members. They lean explicitly into the voluntary and ancillary market, and one of their published examples is a high-turnover client with heavy monthly adjustments — which is basically the DSP profile. They also do divisional billing, splitting invoices by pay group or location with true separate accounting, useful if any of your DSPs run multiple stations. Soluta + 3

2. AdminaHealth — The most established/enterprise option. Their Billing Suite is currently used by 7 of the top 10 U.S. brokers, and it's API-first and cloud-based, covering consolidated invoicing, reconciliation, and payment management. Their "Subconsolidated Bill" module lets you drill down and create individual department, division, or location bills for granular cost allocation — again handy for multi-location DSPs. They integrate with both Employee Navigator and the Ease marketplace, so it slots into common benadmin platforms. Employee Navigator + 2

3. Tabulera — Worth talking to partly because they were exhibiting at the BenefitsPRO Broker Expo (the Chicago show your team went to), so there may already be a warm thread. They offer two models — a lighter version for individual brokers who just want to stay helpful without adding overhead, and a fuller service — so you could start small. A nice feature for your turnover problem: their reconciliation module focuses on timing issues like post-invoice new hires or terminations. And they don't force all your employer groups onto one common HR platform; they integrate with existing systems, which matters across 600 clients. Tabulera + 2

4. Beneration (VerifiaBill) — Strong on the audit/accuracy side. They're system-agnostic and combine invoice consolidation, allocation, and reconciliation built specifically for brokers and carriers. Beyond just consolidating, they audit invoices against your benadmin data to catch billing and coverage discrepancies, and they pair the software with a team of analysts who verify the discrepancies each month. They also offer an optional carrier bill-pay service. Good fit if your bigger pain is premium leakage and error-chasing rather than money movement. BenerationBeneration

5. PlanSource — The outlier, included because it's a real option but a different animal. Their billing reconciliation and payment service audits all list bills, resolves carrier issues, and ensures correct premium payments on your behalf. The catch is it's a module inside a larger benefits-administration platform, so it makes most sense if you'd also consider moving enrollment/benadmin there — otherwise it's heavier than you need just for billing. PlanSource

If I had to point you at

author: Declan McGettigan
body:
@Joshua Luck @Jack Harris Medicare Strategy Call – Summary
Met with "Medicare Mike" (Mike Friedman), an 18-year Medicare veteran, to explore a potential partnership and get guidance on our Medicare launch.
Key takeaways:
Current carrier status: Nearly complete — UHC and HCSC/BCBS still pending through Alrica. Have Wellcare, Aetna, Cigna, Humana, Health Spring, and a couple Med Supp carriers (Mutual of Omaha).
FMO/upline strategy: Mike recommends not consolidating all contracts under one FMO — split eggs across baskets to maintain leverage. Suggests staying with Alrica for now and potentially adding a few separate contracts with Mike for carriers they don't cover.
Carriers to add (Med Supp): Bankers Fidelity, Wellabe, and United American — all available in most/all 50 states.
Advantage vs. Supplement: Call center model favors Medicare Advantage (higher upfront commissions, no underwriting), but retention risk is real as carriers drop commissionable plans. Supplement pays longer-term but requires underwriting. Recommend dedicated back-office retention support for when Advantage plans terminate.
Golden tip: State Pharmaceutical Assistance Programs (SPAPs) can be used as a Special Election Period trigger to enroll clients mid-year outside standard windows.
Compliance note: Call recording discipline is critical — mis-statements on recorded calls have resulted in carrier contract terminations. Mike recommends email-based enrollment disclaimers where possible.
Next steps: Continue with Alrica, evaluate lead quality, and revisit potential release/contract split with Mike if issues arise.

Sharing Recent Quote Timelines for Review

Hello Justin,

Lior mentioned that you and he discussed some of the recent turnaround times on quotes, so I wanted to send over a few concrete examples for visibility.

On the onboarding side, we’ve been seeing about 4 days to get underwriting questions back and roughly a week for firm rates, which is generally what we expect for Q4. No major concerns there.

Most of the delays we’ve experienced have been with our Renewals Team writing new business for Allstate, where the timelines have been noticeably longer. A few examples:

• WES Logistics — firm rates took 10+ days.
• First to You Logistics — 8 days just for underwriting questions; 10+ days total for firm rates.
• Legacy Delivery — bulk apps were submitted, but the file size prevented Allstate from opening it, which caused a significant delay before they could even begin review. After they finally received a readable version, it still took more than a week to get underwriting questions back.

author: Kayla Epstein
body:
Lookback Eligibility Method

Hi Justin,

I hope you’re doing well.

I wanted to connect regarding a process update on our end. We are transitioning to using the IRS-permitted 12-month lookback measurement and stability period to determine full-time eligibility for benefits, rather than assessing status on a strict monthly basis.

Nationwide has recently been requesting pay stubs to validate qualifying life events for employees moving from part-time to full-time status. My concern is that, under the lookback method, an employee may have worked full-time hours in certain prior months but not yet satisfied the full 12-month measurement period required to be considered benefits-eligible. I want to make sure those historical hours do not inadvertently cause a QLE to be declined if they don’t align with our official measurement methodology.

We’d really appreciate the opportunity to align on the best way to handle these situations so that:

* Employees who legitimately satisfy the 12-month measurement criteria can receive coverage without issue, and

* We establish a smooth and consistent validation process that works for both Nationwide and our team.

Would you be available for a quick call to walk through how Nationwide prefers to document and review these cases under the lookback method? We want to ensure our documentation is clear and proactive so there are no unnecessary delays or denials.

Thanks in advance — looking forward to aligning on this.

Best,
Kayla

* 30% census shift threshold:

* Optimyl is leaning toward holding rates as long as there is no more than a ~30% census shift.

* When rates lock in:

* We need to notify Optimyl at the time we send paperwork to the client (after a verbal commitment with the SC).

* Block-of-business review:

* Optimyl is re-looking at our groups as a block of business, with underwriters going through our book to reassess how they’re rating our renewals.

* 90-day trending information:

* Optimyl can let us know ~90 days out where a group is trending, which we can use to:

* Get ahead of challenging renewals
* Plan client conversations earlier
* Decide where we may need to push back or shop more aggressively

body:
Top Line Summary
----------------

* @Lior Goldstein @Kayla Epstein @Jack Harris Optimal will not work with Decisely and will not process BORs that come through them. Our DSP groups cannot get new business quotes via Optimal if Decisely is involved.

* Optimal is in a vetting process with ADP but currently does no insurance business with them. (he dosn't know if or when they will pass)

* Rate lock trigger: we want rates locked when we send PEQ/intake paperwork to the client. We will notify Optimal at that point.

* Renewal review covered several groups: high risk pregnancy, diabetes, mental health, and a few high cost meds driving claims.

* KYNECT expected to need a significant increase, rough indication 20 to 60 percent.

* Turnaround time: commitment remains within 48 hours for renewals on our groups, Aharon will confirm any outliers, especially (seems they are good on this)

* After the first of the year, Optimal plans to send a standardized renewal report prior to these calls.

Renewal Highlights
------------------

* 2/1 AASIRA – 47.9 percent loss ratio, driven mainly by a high risk pregnancy in a small group.

* 2/1 Dukes Logistics – 28.9 percent loss ratio, multiple members with depression, MSK, psoriasis, diabetes, etc, but the top claimant is now off the plan, which may help renewal.

* 2/1 Primetime Logistics – current indication 34.4 percent increase, eleven members with risk increases, multiple chronic conditions, a high cost acne drug (dapson gel, about 710 per month, tier 2), and one major bipolar and coronary claimant now off the plan, renewal may be revisited once removal is confirmed.

* 3/1 Icon – limited experience so far, minimal claims. Initial view is roughly ten percent increase, subject to updated data.

* 3/1 KYNECT – about 15k in the red, high risk pregnancy, cardiac arrhythmia, type 1 diabetes, significant med costs, one severe diabetic risk (Colton Frazier), Optimal’s early view is 20 to 60 percent increase.

Next Steps
----------

Optimal

* @Lior Goldstein @Jack Harris Head actuary to finish renewal framework and send more concrete guidance on renewals, timing may slip a bit because Joseph’s wife is due this week.

* Provide more detail on the high risk pregnancy for AASIRA and how it is being rated.

* Confirm whether Primetime’s current renewal still includes the removed high claimant, and adjust as needed.

* Send complete claims and Rx data for KYNECT and Icon, including detail on high risk diabetic member Colton Frazier.

* Review whether any discount options exist for dapson gel through discount programs, even if it cannot be carved out as specialty.

* Reconfirm internal process to maintain 24–48 hour turnaround for renewals, once Aharon sends examples.

* After the new year, create a standard pre-meeting renewal report to send ahead of these calls.

Innovative

* Communicate internally that Decisely cannot place business with Optimal, and any BOR from Decisely will be blocked, groups must stay with Innovative to stay with Optimal.

* Confirm for Optimal the high cost claimants who have left Dukes and Primetime, especially Leah Pilgrim, so they can re-evaluate renewals.

* For KYNECT, identify the diabetic high risk member and consider offering an individual policy to move that risk off the group plan if the member is willing.

* Confirm the turn around time is good for Renewal business

* Operationally, when we send PEQ or intake paperwork to the client, notify Optimal to lock rates at that point.

* Continue regular renewal cadence meetings with Optimal.

body:
The deal as it was originally presented:

We were told that if we reach 1,000 members on the plan, starting in 2026 we would receive a $100,000 bonus. This was described as a flat, recurring $100,000 every year. The only requirement was maintaining at least 1,000 members on the plan. They do not need to be new members, just a maintained count of 1,000.

At exactly 1,000 members, this equals $100 per member per year, which is $8.3 per member per month.

We were initially excited because the math Gabe walked us through made it appear that our share was a little over one third of total premium. That turned out to be incorrect because the calculation he showed was based on one month of premium instead of 12 months.

author: Declan McGettigan
body:
@Jack Harris Quoting Blockers — Must Be Resolved Before Tool Can Be Used

We cannot effectively quote until the items below are addressed.

P1 — Critical (Blocking Quoting)

1. File Upload/Delete Does Not Persist

File uploads and deletions do not save or reflect correctly.

The issue persists even after page refresh or restarting the workflow.

Impact: Required documentation cannot be trusted or verified.

Required fix: File actions must persist and accurately reflect the correct state at all times.

2. State Does Not Populate in Carrier Emails

The State field does not appear in emails to carriers, even when address or ZIP is entered.

Impact: Incomplete address information prevents accurate carrier quoting.

Required fix: State should auto-populate from address/ZIP and be included in carrier emails.

3. AOR Status Is Not Communicated in Carrier Emails

The AOR toggle (on/off) is not reflected in emails sent to carriers.

Impact: Carriers cannot determine whether we are the Agent of Record, which affects their ability to quote.

Required fix: Carrier emails must clearly state: AOR: Yes or AOR: No.

4. Eligibility Information Does Not Populate in Carrier Emails

Eligibility-related data does not appear in carrier emails, even when entered in the tool.

Impact: Carriers lack required information to assess or quote the case.

Required fix: All entered eligibility information must populate in the carrier email.

5. Missing “Not AOR” Email Disclaimer When AOR = No

When AOR = No, carrier emails do not clarify that information may be limited.

Impact: Carriers may assume missing data is an error rather than a limitation.

Required fix: If AOR = No, carrier emails should include:

“This is all the information we have available at this moment.”

6. Virgin Group Logic Is Missing (Overarching Requirement)

There is no system-level logic to identify a Virgin Group.

Impact: The system requests and expects data that does not exist, blocking accurate quoting.

Required fix: Add a top-level “Virgin Group? — Yes / No” checkpoint.

If Virgin Group = Yes:

Only allow entry of: Address, SIC Code, Effective Date

Disable and do not request: SBCs, Claims, Enrollment, Prior carrier data

Non-Blocking Improvements (After Quoting Works)
P2 — Important but Not Blocking

*7. Hide Empty Fields Instead of Showing “Unknown” (Suggestion)

Fields with no available data currently display “unknown.”

Suggestion: If no data exists, hide the field entirely. (Jack can confirm preferred behavior.)

8. ZIP Validation Message Is Unclear

Current message reads: “ZIP should be address/ZIP.”

Improvement: Replace with clear, user-friendly guidance.

P3 — Nice to Have / Later

9. Census Data Cleanup (Lower Priority)

Census data is not fully normalized.

This can be manually corrected in Excel for now.

Also would like: Automated cleanup in the tool when feasible.

Sending an Email to the Axe RX team asking if it is possible to see how many total app downloads we have had from our groups link. Reason being is that we shared this app with the entire company yet we have had no usage shown. There could be multiple reasons for this but either way this would be important to see.

@Kayla Epstein @Jack Harris @Aharon Heiman I am thinking that if our goal is to really make this something we eventually roll out to all members on our books that it may be better to roll this out to some of our groups and see how much activity we can get. Reason being is we will be able to get more feedback faster that way we can work out the kinks sooner and eventually roll out to our full book of business. Also, even if it doesn't work, there really is no harm done to these groups. What do you think?

If the answer is yes:

I think how we market this is incredibly important. I almost think we should make short but super effective videos and/or flyers to show to members how easy it is to use. Getting members to actually use this will be the hardest, but obviously most important, part. How we market this to owners is also super important, because they have a closer and more present relationship with their employees. If we can sell the idea to owners that, by their employees using this, they will have fewer Rx on their claims, therefore potentially saving them money at renewal, then I think we increase our chances of the message getting through to the employees by a lot.

I am ready to start working on these videos/flyers next week if we decide that trying with some groups is a good idea.

body:
@Jack Harris 

Summary
-------

Initial diligence call with DBL Center (wholesale GA) to evaluate offering Paid Family Leave (PFL) and DBL products across client base. Reviewed underwriting process, implementation requirements, state vs private carrier dynamics, and revenue potential. Currently awaiting competitive quotes across multiple states to determine feasibility.

Key Points
----------

*
PFL is state-mandated in ~13 states; employers default into state plans unless opting into private carriers

*
Private carriers can offer:
*
Competitive or lower rates vs state

*
Better service, claims visibility, and reporting

*
Pricing driven by payroll, demographics, and industry risk

*
Implementation requires:
*
Carrier placement + application

*
State exemption filing (employer involvement required)

*
State-specific deadlines (e.g., CO May 1) create near-term urgency

Financial Impact
----------------

*
Estimated premium block: ~$2M–$3M

*
Commission: ~10% (non-NY states)

*
Estimated annual revenue: ~$200K+
*
Paid quarterly

Risks / Considerations
----------------------

*
Carrier appetite not guaranteed (some already declined)

*
Multi-state complexity and varying compliance rules

*
NJ particularly admin-heavy

*
Some clients may already be privately insured

*
Requires internal onboarding + education process

Next Steps
----------

*
Receive and review carrier quotes (expected this week)

*
Analyze savings vs state plans

*
Determine % of clients that can realistically convert

*
Schedule follow-up call with DBL Center

*
Decide go/no-go and build rollout process if viable

Notes
-----

body:
Update 3/26

Created a Google Form to collect all the information needed from individuals in the company to view what states they are and are not licensed in through Sircon. Sent this out to most licensed individuals and began building a spreadsheet to identify gaps.

After putting together a full view spreadsheet of licenses across all 50 states, it became clear we were missing a significant number of licenses, with many expired. Nearly everyone (outside of agency licenses) were missing multiple states.

Got approval from Kayla to move forward with getting all RSs and enrollers licensed. Sent out a new Google Form specifically for initial licensing (since it requires more information than renewals), and coordinated with Raquel's team on processing the payments to get everything submitted.

For states that require additional steps after payment (e.g., Florida fingerprinting, Georgia notarized forms, etc.), I created a PDF outlining all required action items with instructions and direct links for completion. This can now be reused for anyone getting licensed in the future.

In parallel, I’ve been working on a Replit app to track all licenses across the company in one dashboard. It is already usable and close to complete. However, on 3/26, I found a way to connect everyone’s individual Sircon accounts to Innovative’s agency Sircon account. This allows us to track all licenses across the company in all 50 states directly through the agency login, including a dashboard for active and expiring licenses.

I spent the latter half of the day today 3/26 setting up these connections and sent out instructions for individuals to accept their invites. This will be a game changer and will likely replace both the spreadsheet and the need for a separate app, as Sircon now provides a more efficient and centralized solution. The spreadsheet ultimately served its purpose in identifying the initial gaps. But these Sircon connections will be what we use moving forward. @Declan McGettigan @Jack Harris

Jack and I had the three-way meeting today with Beam (Alex) and Breakpoint (Valentina, Trent, Grant). Really good energy in the room — both sides are bought in and actively want to make this work.

Short version: Beam stays the client-facing experience for dental/vision (zero disruption at renewal — same Beam card, same portal, same everything). Breakpoint brings the captive and the voluntary benefit products on their ERISA chassis. We sit in the middle taking risk through the captive. Life and disability stay fully insured as-is for now.

The captive is a protected cell in Tennessee under Acrisure, and the self-capitalization structure means no extra dollars out-of-pocket on top of premium. Beam's book is running a mid-50s loss ratio, so there's real underwriting profit to go after.

Assuming the numbers work, this is the path Jack and I would recommend. Next step is pulling all the economics together side-by-side from both partners so you can see the full picture and sign off on the path and on risk levels if you want to go this way.

Full meeting notes attached. BeamBreakpoint_ThreeWay_Meeting_Notes_2026-04-20.pdf

author: Aharon Heiman
body:
Great meeting today — recap, next steps, and kicking off a group chain

Hi all,

Thanks for a really productive call today. Putting everyone on the same chain as promised so scheduling the next meeting is easier from here.

Jack and I came away feeling great about the direction. The structure we talked through feels like the right fit for all three sides, and we appreciate how flexible both Beam and Breakpoint were in working through the details.

Quick recap of where we landed:

• Beam stays the client and member-facing experience — MGU and front/back-end TPA for dental and vision, front-end TPA for everything, system of record for eligibility.
• Breakpoint manages the captive and brings the supplemental benefits (accident, CI, hospital indemnity) on the ERISA chassis, plus back-end TPA and claims on the supps.
• Life and disability stay fully insured through Beam as they are today.
• Innovative takes risk through the captive, percentage TBD pending the numbers.

Next step is getting the economics side-by-side so we can build out the full business plan. Here's what we each have to send over:

From our side (Innovative):
• A full report on our supp health book — lives, premium, plan designs, including carriers outside of Beam.
• Our growth and additional volume expectations to help Alex size the opportunity internally.

From Beam (Alex):
• Take a first stab at outlining the economics line items across all product lines so we can all fill in our numbers and work backwards from a common framework.
• Claims data and performance on our groups over the past couple of years — book-level loss ratio breakdown plus group-specific history.
• Beam's current risk positions on dental, vision, life, and the reasoning behind them — especially as they apply to our groups.
• An internal read on build time and what needs to be built on the Beam side to support the captive / ERISA chassis and the supp eligibility push to Breakpoint.

From Breakpoint (Trent / Valentina / Grant):
• Plan specs on the voluntary benefits, over to Alex.
• Captive financials (reserves, overall position) so we have the full picture of the structure.

Once everyone has what they need, we'll get a follow-up working session on the calendar focused specifically on economics. If there's anything we missed or anything you need from us to move your piece forward, just reply all and we'll get it going.

Thanks again, looking forward to where this goes!

--

author: Kayla Epstein
body:
*****************************************************************
Roadmap: Building an Innovative-Owned Ancillary Risk Program (Dental / Vision / Accident / CI / HI)
*****************************************************************

1. Internal Readiness
---------------------

* Confirm total premium volume, membership counts, and risk tolerance.
* Build internal actuarial model for expected loss ratios + volatility ranges.
* Define which product lines we want in phase 1 (Dental/Vision) and phase 2 (Accident/CI/Hospital).

2. MGU + Carrier Structure
--------------------------

* Identify MGUs that offer quota-share structures and allow Innovative to hold risk day one.
* Select a fronting carrier for policy issuance (A-rated).
* Determine % risk share, admin fees, and carrier fronting fee.
* Lock in reserving requirements and collateral obligations.

3. Network & Claims Infrastructure
--------------------------------------

* Evaluate dental/vision networks (multiple options).
* Select claims engine + adjudication rules.
* Set up data feeds, EDI, eligibility files, and system integration.

4. Product & Pricing Development
------------------------------------

* Build plan designs tailored to our block.
* MGU + actuary finalize rates, underwriting rules, and renewal methodology.
* Build rating tools and quoting workflow for our internal teams.

5. Operational Build
--------------------

* Create enrollment + billing + agent comp workflows.
* Establish customer service, claims escalation, and reporting processes.
* Build dashboards: premium flow, claims performance, LR tracking, reserve levels.

6. Compliance & Regulatory
------------------------------

* Set up reinsurance/quota-share agreements.
* Confirm state filings, carrier approvals, and ERISA/ACA interactions.
* Establish data security + HIPAA compliance for the program.

7. Financial & Reinsurance Structure
----------------------------------------

* Define quota-share % (e.g., 25% → 50% → 75%).
* Secure stop-loss or aggregate protection (if desired).
* Set up trust accounts, reserves, and reporting cadence.

8. Launch & Scaling
-----------------------

* Pilot with a portion of our existing groups.
* Monitor early LR performance, adjust pricing/benefits as needed.
* Expand across full book and into broker distribution.

Link to Spreadsheet with the numbers

author: Aharon Heiman
body:
Voluntary benefits program structure and partnership discussion

Hi Darren,

I hope you are doing well. I wanted to reach out with a concept we have been actively exploring and get your initial reaction before we go any further. Given our existing relationship and the amount of voluntary benefit business we already place with The Standard, we felt it made sense to have this conversation with you.

At a high level, we are looking to evolve beyond a traditional commission-only distribution model and participate directly in the risk on voluntary benefits. The goal is to build proprietary program (and maybe plan designs) where we retain meaningful economic interest, while partnering with a strong carrier for fronting, paper, and certain operational functions.

The lines we are focused on are the core voluntary suite, dental, vision, accident, critical illness, hospital indemnity, Life, and short-term disability.

Our current working concept looks like this:

• We would form a captive structure that allows us to retain a defined portion of the risk and underwriting results.
• We would design and control the plan architecture, pricing philosophy, and distribution strategy. (with you we may not need to do that and we can just white label your current plan designs)
• We would partner with a carrier to act as the fronting carrier, issuing the policies and supporting regulatory and filing requirements.
• Administrative responsibilities could be flexible, either largely carrier-led, shared, or split by line of coverage, depending on what is most practical.
• Over time, we would expect to function in a program manager-style role, with accountability for performance, growth, and risk outcomes.

We are already placing a significant amount of voluntary benefits business today, and this initiative is about aligning our long-term incentives more closely with the programs we are building and distributing. Said another way, we are looking to have more ownership in the outcomes rather than only participating at the distribution layer.

One important clarification is that the structure outlined above represents the general framework we are building, but it was not designed around any one carrier relationship. Much of this was assuming we would be building this from the ground up. Being that we are good with your current offerings we may not need to do that so while the core concept would remain consistent, the exact mechanics of how this would work with The Standard could look different in practice.

Separately, this is an initiative we fully intend to move forward with. As part of this program, our expectation would be to consolidate participation across our book, including existing groups, future growth, and lines that are not currently placed with you today. For context, our dental and vision premium alone exceeds $5 million at present, and that business is included in the scope of what we are looking to bring under this type of structure. The purpose of this conversation is simply to understand whether this is something The Standard would want to participate in, before we move ahead with another partner.

At this stage, we are not looking for detailed terms, pricing, or commitments. What would be most helpful is understanding whether this is directionally something The Standard would be open to exploring, and if so, who on your side would be the right stakeholders to involve in a higher-level discussion.

If it would be helpful, we are happy to put together a short, structured overview that you could share internally to frame the opportunity and the ask.

Let me know your thoughts when you have a chance, and happy to walk through this live, if that is easier.

Happy Holidays!

--

Aharon Heiman

Benefits Analyst

Innovative Benefits (A Pinecrest Company)

18111 Preston Road | Suite 600 | Dallas, Texas 75252

Office: 469-356-5169 | Direct: 848-285-7178

Email: aharon@innovativebps.com

author: Jack Harris
body:
Spoke with Gary — productive conversation. Main topics:

* LLH / staffing conference

Gary recommended we connect directly with the main people at LLH (Live Life Healthy), not just through downstream distributors. Kevin from Malaysian is connected to them. LLH is doing major Section 125 business, including with stadium groups, and will have a major presence at the SIA Staffing Conference in Austin (24th–26th). Gary strongly recommended someone from our team attend. Even if LLH dominates the 125 angle there, the conference could still be a strong opportunity for GHI + Workers’ Comp, especially with staffing firms in higher-risk industries like warehousing/construction. - I think we may want to consider sending a sales guy there if anyone wants to go.

* PEOs + Section 125

Discussed how different PEOs handle Section 125 differently. Gary said Peoplease will not allow it, while E3 actually sells it. He also mentioned Samuel Hale (CA VO) said some PEOs in California will not allow 125 and will not treat it as a wage reduction, because they operate under contract law, not insurance law, so they can define that in their agreements.

* WOTC opportunity

Discussed WOTC (Work Opportunity Tax Credit) as a possible opportunity for DSPs due to high churn/turnover. Asked Gary if he knows any potential contacts/partners in that space. He said he’ll think about it and get back to me.

* NY State Fund Workers’ Comp

Gary explained the New York State Fund can be extremely compelling for qualifying groups: client gets 30% upfront discount + ~30% backend dividend; our comp is 6% upfront + 5% backend = ~11% total. Qualification is generally based on mod score of 1.0 or better. If a group qualifies, this is basically a no-brainer and impossible for other carriers to compete with. Dazose mentioned we already have 4 clients signed with the State Fund.

Action items:

* Build targeted NY DSP lists (clients + prospects) and have reps proactively sell the NY State Fund / ask for mod scores.

* Build targeted CA lists (clients + prospects) and pitch 125 + Workers’ Comp savings together.

* Casey said he will start putting together the list(s).

* Do a training / powwow with the sales team so they understand the NY State Fund opportunity well.

* potentially send a sales guy to the SIA conferenc

* @Lior Goldstein @Casey Hickey

author: Samuel Ghirmay
body:
Hi Aharon,

I’ve reviewed the agreement and wanted to flag a few items for discussion and possible clarification or revision. None of these are deal-breakers, but addressing them now should help avoid ambiguity or issues down the road.

Payments Section

•	It would be helpful to clarify what happens if there are issues with setting fees or if the NEXBEN program is not functioning as expected. Some contingency language here may reduce uncertainty. (Low priority)

•	The agreement currently provides a 180-day window to dispute payments. We should consider whether this is sufficient or whether a longer period would be more appropriate to avoid potential reconciliation issues. (Moderate priority)

Agreement Updates / Amendments

•	As drafted, this section appears to give eHealth fairly broad discretion to update terms and rates. We may want to add protective language along the lines of:

“provided that any change that materially and adversely affects Broker’s rights or finances shall not be effective without Broker’s written consent.” (moderate-high priority)

•	If we anticipate open and ongoing communication, I don’t believe the additional 30-day notice language I suggested is strictly necessary, assuming you don’t foresee this being an issue. (Low)

No Navigator Standard

•	I added language at the end of this clause to prevent potential misuse of the provision:

“…provided that eHealth shall provide prompt written notice to Broker and an opportunity to demonstrate non-Navigator status before withholding or ceasing payments.”

•	That said, we can remove this if you believe it introduces unnecessary risk or friction. (Low)

Agent of Record

•	We may want to clarify Broker’s role by adding language such as:

“…however, Broker will be recognized as the broker of record for purposes of employer group relationship management and will have access, subject to Applicable Law, to enrollment status and commission reporting necessary to administer Broker Monthly Fees.” (Low - might not be necessary if you dont think it will be an issue)

Limitation of Liability

•	The limitation of liability provision is fairly broad. We should consider carving out exceptions for willful misconduct and breaches of confidentiality or data/security obligations. (Moderate)

Dispute Resolution / Governing Law

•	Please note that the agreement includes binding arbitration, with Texas law governing. I would recommend adding venue language specifying Texas as well, though this is not a high-priority issue. (Low)

Let me know your thoughts on the above and which items you’d like to pursue further. Happy to revise language or prioritize based on your preferences.

Best regards,

Samuel

IC Production_Broker ICHRA Referral and Administration Agreement Redline changes.docx

*****************************************

These are the dominant boards used across the trucking industry.

---------------------------

* The largest load board in the U.S.
* Used by ~70–80% of brokers and carriers.

2. Truckstop.com
----------------

* The second-largest load board.
* Strong for spot freight, flatbed, and reefer.

* Digital freight marketplace.
* Carriers book loads directly through the app.

------------------------------------------

* One of the largest brokers; posts thousands of loads daily.

* Major digital board with direct shipper freight.

*******************************

Still widely used but not as dominant as DAT/Truckstop.

author: Aharon Heiman
body:
@Kayla Epstein 

Here are 3 example rewrites so you can see the direction. I'll show the current version vs the new version for each:

Deductible — Analogy
--------------------

Current:

Think of the deductible like the cover charge at a club. Before the insurance company will "buy you a drink" (pay for your care), you have to pay the cover yourself. Once you've paid the cover, the club starts splitting the tab with you — but you're not drinking for free yet. That's coinsurance.

New:

Think of the deductible like a security deposit on a delivery van. Before the leasing company covers any repairs, you pay the first chunk yourself. Once you've hit that amount, they start splitting costs with you — but you're not off the hook completely until you hit your OOP max. That's when they cover everything.

Deductible — Practical
----------------------

Current:

In most plan designs, deductibles apply when there's no flat copay for a service — but some plans require the deductible to be met even before a copay kicks in, so always verify on the SBC...

New:

Most of our DSP groups are on a $3,500 deductible plan with copays. That means a DA can walk into urgent care, pay their copay, and never think about the deductible — until they need something bigger like an ER visit or imaging. Then the deductible hits hard. Because our workforce is young and healthy, most DAs won't hit their deductible in a given year — which is why these plans are priced lower. But when a DA calls confused about a $2,000 ER bill, this is exactly what happened: their deductible kicked in. Know the plan, check the SBC, and walk them through the math.

Level Funded — Practical
------------------------

Current:

Level funded tends to work best for smaller employer groups with a younger, healthier workforce — where the medical underwriting works in the group's favor...

New:

This is our bread and butter. Almost every DSP we write is level funded — and for good reason. Our groups are young, healthy, and male-dominant, which means underwriting works in their favor. The carriers we use most (Nationwide, Optimal, Angle, SmartCare) all offer level-funded products designed for exactly this profile. The DSP pays a fixed monthly amount, and if claims come in low (which they usually do with 20% enrollment of healthy employees), the group can get money back. The catch: if a group has even one bad claimant, the renewal can spike hard. And because participation is low, one sick member has an outsized impact on the claims fund. That's why underwriting matters — and why we sometimes lose groups that picked up a high-risk employee mid-year.

author: Jack Harris
body:
Questions and answers from Ensure below. There are a few new questions that arise at of this:



1. Can groups BOR away from us?
-------------------------------

Answer: Of course—groups can always BOR to go to competitors, etc. They will not BOR to Ensura since we are offering supplementary benefits that do not require a BOR.

2. How exactly do you integrate into their payroll so that the benefit premium and claim payout show up on the paystub?
-----------------------------------------------------------------

Answer: For DSPs we have Paycom and ADP dialed in. It’s a process with each payroll where they get payroll ready to send. Then they send us a file that we modify and return back to them. They upload that file to ADP or Paycom as it includes the premium and claim payment. ADP is working with us on fully automating the process via API—this will be ready in January.

3. I watched the video you sent, and it said employees pay for the plan. However, I thought it was fully employer-paid?
-----------------------------------------------------------------

Answer: Employers do have a fee. It is $38 per employee per month. This is billed after the payroll tax savings are achieved. Typical savings is $105 saved on payroll tax, so after the $38 fee, they still net $67 in savings.

So yes, there is a cost, but the savings far exceed the cost—resulting in no out-of-pocket expense.

4. Who is writing the health coaching newsletter? What criteria must be included?
-----------------------------------------------------------------

Answer: Medical professionals from Amaze Health write it. All content is about health screening and being proactive about healthcare.

5. Can you break down how the tiered system works? Most DSP employees fall into the $1,200 plan—how does that math work and how does it affect take-home pay?
-----------------------------------------------------------------

Answer: Each employee is unique with deductions and pre-tax items. We have an algorithm that places them into the highest plan possible while still ensuring their take-home pay increases.

6. Isn’t it “suspicious” that an accident plan costs over $1,000 when major medical premiums are not that expensive?
-----------------------------------------------------------------

Answer: This is not a concern. The plan is fully compliant and many insurance plans exceed $1k monthly. If anyone is audited, our compliance process covers that—we pay the fees required to prove compliance.

7. Do you have a letter of recommendation from a law firm endorsing this setup?
-----------------------------------------------------------------

body:
SmartHealth+ Legal Opinions

Hi Eric,

First off thank you for your response regarding the BOR policy.

I read through the documents you sent over and have a few comments.

Regarding the 2nd document titled "SmartHealth+ tax analysis":

1. Key Definition: Excess Benefit
It states in section 1 that excess benefit is subject to FICA, federal, and state taxes. Our understanding is that while excess benefits may be includable in gross income, FICA applies only to wages (IRC §3121). Fixed indemnity benefits, even when taxable, are generally treated as income rather than wages, and therefore would be subject to income tax but not FICA. - This is further confirmed in the CCA legal response document.

2. Employee Contribution Treatment
The example references a $200/month employee contribution while also indicating that the full premium is paid pre-tax through a Section 125 cafeteria plan. Under §125, salary reduction amounts used to purchase accident and health coverage are treated as employer contributions for tax purposes. As such, it is unclear how a portion of the premium would simultaneously be treated as an employee contribution.

Relatedly, because premiums paid through §125 are treated as employer-funded, our understanding is that they would not qualify as employee-paid medical expenses under IRC §213(d) for purposes of offsetting taxable benefits.

Regarding the first document titled "CCA legal response":

We agree with just about everything in this document.

It mentions on pg 7 that "SATISFIED: SH+ enrollment materials and plan documents clearly explain that if indemnity benefits received exceed actual unreimbursed medical expenses in a tax year, the “excess” may be includible in gross income on the employee’s personal tax return (per Rev. Rul. 69-154). Employees acknowledge this disclosure." - Can you please point us to where to find that disclosure in the SmartHealth+ marketing materials?

Thank you,

author: Jack Harris
body:
MGU / Program Manger for Ancillary Products

Hey Joe,

At a high level, we are exploring becoming our own MGU / Program Administrator for ancillary products, including dental, vision, accident, critical illness, hospital indemnity, short-term disability, and life. We’ve had initial conversations with consulting firms, carriers, and others in this space, and we want to sanity-check our thinking with someone who has deep actuarial and carrier-side experience.

Our current understanding of the likely structure is as follows:

* We would want to retain as much risk as possible, which likely means forming a captive (or similar risk-bearing entity) capable of participating on the reinsurance side.

* From there, we see two primary paths:

* White-labeling an existing carrier’s products and negotiating a reinsurance arrangement, or

* Filing our own rates, developing our own actuarial rate tables, and running a proprietary program while using a fronting carrier to write on their paper.

* Based on preliminary discussions with carriers, it appears that the second option—owning the rates and program design and using a front—may be more attractive from a long-term economics and control standpoint.

* We also recognize the need for TPA relationships to handle policy administration, claims, billing, etc., and our assumption is that most of that would be outsourced rather than built internally.

* Separately, we understand there are additional layers around networks (where applicable), compliance, state filings, capital requirements, and governance that need to be thoughtfully structured.

We’re still very much in the early discovery phase, and before going too far down any one path, I’d really value your thoughts on:

* Whether this framing generally aligns with how you’ve seen successful programs built

* Key components or risks we may be underestimating or overlooking

* Actuarial or regulatory considerations that tend to surprise groups at this stage

* Any guidance on sequencing (what needs to be solved first vs. later)

Thanks so much, looking forward to connecting.

Jack Harris

body:
Follow up and clarification question

Hi Larry,

Thank you again for taking the time to walk us through the program with us. We really appreciate the depth you went into, particularly around the program outline, opinion letters and legal memos from your counsel. It is clear a lot of thought and work went into putting the structure together.

As we understand it, the structure is designed to provide a legitimate benefits plan with real coverage and services, while also being structured in a way that, through tax savings, does not ultimately cost the employer or employee out of pocket dollars.

Based on your example, our understanding is that the employer pays $755, the employee receives $625 back as a claim payment, and that entire $625 is not treated as wages, and therefore avoids FICA. Of that $625, $150 is treated not only as non wage income, but as other income that is deductible, on the basis that it represents reimbursement for an incurred qualified medical expense.

Assuming all of that is correct, we are aligned that the overall economics can work.

The one meaningful remaining question we are struggling to reconcile is specifically around the $150 incurred medical expense under a Section 125 structure. Because this program is implemented through a Section 125 plan and the premium is treated as employer paid, we are having difficulty understanding how the employee is deemed to have an incurred qualified medical expense in this scenario. From our perspective, the employee never actually pays out of pocket for the coverage, and there does not appear to be an unreimbursed expense borne by the employee prepaid or otherwise.

We would appreciate any additional clarification you can provide on how that $150 qualifies as an incurred qualified medical expense to the employee in this structure, particularly given the Section 125 treatment.

Thanks again for your time, and we appreciate you walking us through the program.

Best,

--

author: Samuel Ghirmay
body:
Hi @Aharon Heiman ,

Here are the key issues and revisions I identified during my review of the agreement:

* Section D (No Copying / Reverse Engineering / Derivatives): I clarified that this restriction applies solely to the confidential information received from them.

* Section 3(C) – Improvements: I limited the assignment language so it does not apply to any pre-existing intellectual property or independently developed improvements.

* Section 4 – Survival: I modified the survival period to align with industry standards—three (3) years—except for trade secrets, which will survive for as long as they remain protected as such.

* Non-Circumvention: I significantly tightened this provision to ensure that independently sourced or pre-existing clients are not implicated or considered a breach of the NDA.

* Liability Limitation: I added language benefiting the disclosing party to avoid onerous liabilities or impermissible penalties.

* Term and Termination: I left these provisions unchanged, as either party may terminate the agreement with written notice.

There are two additional items we discussed that I can revise if you think it’s worthwhile, though I don’t view them as critical:

1. Making certain clauses mutual—although it’s unclear whether they will be receiving meaningful confidential information from us.
2. Reiterating the non-circumvention and survival limitations within the termination section by reducing the five-year period to three years and clarifying that survival is governed by Section 6. This would be largely duplicative, but I can clean it up if helpful. If this is confusing, I’m happy to walk through it over the phone.

Lastly, could you point out which sections you initially found most onerous? I want to make sure I’m evaluating the agreement through the same lens you are.

Thanks, and let me know if you have any questions.

Best regards,
Samuel Ghirmay.

Revised the post-termination commission and fee provisions to clarify that Producer remains entitled to all commissions and fees on Products sold or Clients originated by Producer prior to termination (including renewals and trails), provided termination is not for uncured material breach involving fraud or willful misconduct; deleted language limiting commissions to only Products GP is currently servicing and removed references to Producer's role in "initial sale and promotion."

Indemnification

Rewrote and narrowed the mutual indemnification obligations: (a) deleted Capstone, Century, Kingston, and third-party insurance carriers as indemnified parties; (b) specified Producer only indemnifies GP for third-party claims arising from Producer's gross negligence, willful misconduct, or material breach; (c) required GP to provide reciprocal indemnification to Producer for claims arising from GP or affiliates’ gross negligence, willful misconduct, or material breach, and removed broad language regarding all damages, liabilities, and expenses.

Limitation of Liability

Clarified the carve-out from limitation of liability, now expressly referencing “liability that cannot be limited by law,” “each party’s indemnification obligations,” “breach of confidentiality,” and “infringement or misappropriation of intellectual property," and deleted less specific references to certain section numbers.

Regulatory Termination Rights

Deleted GP’s right to immediately and without notice terminate or modify the Agreement based on its own determination of interests or compliance risk to affiliated parties, replacing it with a requirement for at least 30 days' prior written notice and narrowing the right to circumstances where GP reasonably determines changes are legally required, and clarified that such modification cannot retroactively reduce Producer’s accrued compensation.

Overall Summary

The redlines primarily narrow and clarify the parties’ rights and obligations upon termination, mutual indemnification, and liability, making compensation and indemnity more reciprocal and predictable, and impose procedural safeguards—including notice and protection of earned compensation—on GP’s right to modify or terminate the agreement for regulatory compliance. The revisions also remove former affiliate references and ambiguous language, focusing the agreement more tightly on the defined parties.



After you look at this lets chat on the phone before you move forward.



Best regards,

Sammie Ghirmay.GP AGENCY, INC - Producer agreement Redline.docx

author: Aharon Heiman
body:
Met with Breckpoint, TPA, MGU, captive manager. They act as TPA for voluntary benefits and non-major medical. They do not TPA major medical, but can MGU and underwrite any line. They operate multiple captives, including member-owned captives where underwriting surplus flows back to participating employers.

Primary differentiator is their ability to structure level-funded voluntary benefits through a captive. This allows uniform plan design across all states, materially simpler compliance, no fronting carrier, and no state-by-state form filings. Approval is primarily tied to the captive domicile, making rollout faster, cheaper, and operationally simpler, with identical VB plans nationwide.

Two captive paths discussed:

• Cell within their existing captive, no upfront $250k capitalization. Risk is effectively ~$1 per $5 of premium, but in practice this is largely supported by premium flow once the program is running, with pooled capital at the master captive level covering early risk.

• Standalone captive we own, which would require ~$250,000 in initial capital contribution.

Illustrative economics per $100 premium: ~$25 broker, ~$30 fixed costs (MGU, underwriting, TPA, admin), ~$45 variable (claims). In their 2024 captive results, only ~$10 of the $45 variable was paid in claims, leaving significant underwriting surplus and profit participation potential.

Overall takeaway: compelling captive-based VB model with national uniformity, dramatically reduced compliance friction, no fronting carrier costs, and meaningful margin upside, especially when leveraging their existing captive structure.

Initial Implementation Call Structure

Hi Kyle,

Thank you so much for all your hard work on these implementation calls. We’re really excited to get this rolling.

I wanted to share some feedback from our sales team regarding the initial call.

Since our reps are already giving employers a fairly robust overview of the plan before any contracts are signed, it may be more efficient if the first implementation call does not revisit the basic plan details, as that can feel a bit redundant. author: Samuel Ghirmay
body:
Hello guys, sorry for the delay. Here is my review of this contract and changes I made. The highlighted portions are just to make sure you are good with the fee terms stated.

Section 3.01 – Administrative Fee
Expanded the definition of the compensation period to include renewals, extensions, replacements, expansions, or re-contracts with Clients or their Affiliates, whether entered directly or indirectly.

Section 3.07 – TPA Fee Payment
Clarified that TPA payments to ASSOCIATE are credited against, but not in lieu of, Smart Workforce’s Fee obligation, and specified that Smart Workforce remains responsible for any deficiency or nonpayment by the TPA.

Section 4.03 – Confidentiality
Deleted language restricting disclosure of Smart Workforce’s Confidential Information by ASSOCIATE only, replaced with a mutual restriction (“neither party”), and revised the permitted recipients of Confidential Information to include Affiliates, employees, contractors, and advisors with a need to know and confidentiality obligations no less protective than in the Agreement; deletions removed references to Clients, Leads, invoices, and payments as part of the scope, but permitted general disclosure of the existence of the agreement and nature of the relationship.

Section 5.04 – ASSOCIATE Indemnification
Revised the indemnification provisions by (i) clarifying the scope to claims from third parties arising directly from ASSOCIATE's gross negligence or willful misconduct or material breach of this Agreement, (ii) removing indemnification obligations for actions of TPA, Vendor, or HCI, and (iii) deleting indemnification for violations of intellectual property or proprietary rights involving the Programs or Vendor services.

Section 7.05 – Assignment
Broadened the assignment provision to apply to assignment of rights or obligations by either party (not just Smart Workforce), require prior written consent not to be unreasonably withheld but allow assignments to Affiliates or in connection with a merger, acquisition, reorganization, or asset sale without such consent, and clarified that any assignment does not relieve Smart Workforce of its obligations unless assumed in writing by the assignee; several restrictive references and assignment formalities were deleted.

Overall Summary
These changes clarify, expand, and make more reciprocal key provisions around fee payment, confidentiality, indemnification, and assignment. Several restrictive obligations and references were deleted, while additional language broadens rights for both parties and clarifies obligation triggers, especially relating to indemnification and assignment.



Let me know if you have any other concerns.



Best regards,

Sammie Ghirmay.

Hi Aharon, I have looked at this agreement it is still pretty similar with some changes. I have done a few more redlines just to be cautious as I am not sure why they changed it up. let me know what you think, here is what I changed;



Section 2.01 Representation

Added that the Associate’s right to advertise, promote, and market the Programs is non-exclusive in addition to nontransferable.

Section 2.02 Designation

Deleted the limitation that Lead designation is “only” for ninety days and replaced it with a one hundred eighty (180) day term, subject to tolling during active negotiations, and clarified the possibility of written re-designation.

Section 2.09 – Marketing Conflict

Added that the restriction on contacting parties identified as leads only applies if Smart Workforce provides such identification in writing and the restriction does not apply to Self-Generated Business.

Section 3.01 Administrative Fee

Added that Fees remain payable regardless of amendments, renewals, replacements, or expansions with the Client or its Affiliates; upon termination for convenience with good standing, added continued Fee payment including for renewals/extensions; modified forfeiture upon termination for cause to apply only with respect to Clients directly and proximately affected by the breach (rather than immediate, global forfeiture); deleted language implying immediate or universal forfeiture of Fees upon cause.

Section 3.07 – TPA Fee Payment

Added that duplicative Fee payments from a TPA are in lieu of Smart Workforce’s obligations, with Smart Workforce remaining secondarily liable if the TPA fails to pay owed amounts.

Section 5.02 - Limitation of Damages

Added exception specifying that the limitation on consequential, exemplary, incidental, or punitive damages does not apply to indemnification, breach of confidentiality, willful misconduct, or fraud.

Section 5.04 - ASSOCIATE Indemnification

Extensively revised and narrowed indemnification: deleted broad obligation to indemnify multiple parties for all liabilities/demands/actions, replaced with indemnification of Vendors from third-party claims arising specifically from ASSOCIATE’s gross negligence, willful misconduct, or material breach of contract.

Section 5.05 - Smart Workforce Indemnification

Revised Smart Workforce’s indemnity to ASSOCIATE to cover third-party claims (rather than general liabilities) arising specifically from Smart Workforce’s gross negligence, willful misconduct, or material breach, including IP infringement claims related to Programs or Marketing Materials.

Section 5.08 – Referrals

Shortened post-termination referral restriction period from twelve to six months, narrowed scope to soliciting for the primary purpose of diverting Clients/Leads from the Programs, excluded Self Generated Business, clarified restriction applies only to Clients/Leads with whom ASSOCIATE had direct contact in the prior twelve months, and deleted the restriction on programs/service offerings and indirect solicitation.

Section 6.01 – Termination

Added that Smart Workforce’s termination for convenience does not relieve it of Fee payment obligations, including tail commissions.

Section 6.03 - Cancellation for Cause

Revised forfeiture of future Fees upon cause termination: deleted immediate and universal forfeiture, now applying only as specified in Section 3.01 and solely for Clients directly and proximately affected by the breach.

Section 7.05 - Assignment

Added that Smart Workforce’s assignment of the contract does not relieve its obligations unless the assignee expressly assumes all obligations in writing and has financial capacity.



Best regards,

Sammie.

body:
@Aharon Heiman Here are some redlines I made on this contract. As always you can reject any changes you don't think are important before you send it off.



Commissions and Compensation
Added a restriction that changes to the commission schedule (Addendum A) cannot reduce Producer’s compensation for business already sold, bound, or in implementation; clarified that offset and repayment of commissions only occur in specific circumstances and proportionally, and limited obligations to "undisputed" and "due and owing" indebtedness, while deleting broader offset language for all debt.

Commission Offset and Repayment
Revised to clarify that commission offsets or obligations to return commissions/fees by Producer/Sub-Agent apply only to amounts directly attributable to refunded amounts and only if such refunds are actually made; deleted broader language requiring any returned fee/commission to be repaid or offset for all cases.

TKBIS's Rights Regarding Commission Changes
Added a requirement for thirty (30) days’ prior written notice before making any changes to commissions due to carrier-imposed alterations or other circumstances, and limited application of such changes prospectively so as not to reduce compensation on existing or in-process business; deleted language permitting retroactive reductions.

Producer Performance and Indemnity
Limited Producer’s responsibility for costs and attorney fees to cases involving negligence, willful misconduct, or breach by Producer/Sub-Agent, expressly excluding damages caused by TKBIS, and required that indemnified costs be reasonable and actually incurred; deleted prior blanket language for "all" such costs.

Marketing Materials
Added that injunctive relief for misuse of marketing materials or names/logos is subject to applicable law and equitable defenses; replaced responsibility for "all damages" with responsibility for "direct" damages, specified "reasonable" fees and actual costs, and changed several references from "all" to "direct" or "reasonable" to narrow exposure; deleted and replaced language to clarify causation and scope of responsibility.

Confidentiality and Non-Solicitation
Added customary exceptions for required disclosures by law, court order, or client consent to confidentiality obligations; revised and clarified non-solicitation to cover a twelve (12) month period post-termination.

PHMP Program Components and Pricing
Replaced TKBIS’s "sole and absolute discretion" with "reasonable discretion exercised in good faith" regarding PHMP Program component and pricing decisions; now requires at least thirty (30) days’ prior written notice for changes (deleted "any time without" notice), and expressly prohibits Producer/Sub-Agents from adding/selling products without prior written approval.

Liquidated Damages
Clarified that any violation by Producer/Sub-Agents triggers payment of the amount of direct administrative fees or commissions actually lost by TKBIS or KBA as liquidated damages, not as a penalty, clarifying and narrowing previous language.

Proposal Compensation Changes
Deleted language allowing immediate effectiveness of compensation revisions, instead requiring not less than thirty (30) days’ prior written notice and stating such changes will only have prospective effect and not affect pre-existing business.

Refunded Fees
Restricted Agent’s loss of commission rights only to the extent of "refunded amounts," rather than any fees, and required repayment "provided such refund is actually made"; deleted broader obligations and clarified the basis for offset or direct payment demands.

Indemnity – Agent’s Obligations
Limited Agent's indemnity for third-party claims to the extent arising from Agent’s negligence, willful misconduct, or breach (rather than "any nature whatsoever" under the agreement or for all acts of agents/employees), and excluded extent caused by the Corporation; deleted overbroad causation and responsibility language.

body:
AB Dashboard .html You have to download it and then open it. @Jack Harris @Lior Goldstein @Rivka Simon

The scoring criteria: 

Each AB is scored out of 100 points across four categories. Every category is relative, meaning the top performer in that category gets the max possible points, and everyone else is scored proportionally against them.

Category
Weight
Max Points

Meetings Set
35%
35 pts

Dial + Talk Time
25%
25 pts

Dials
20%
20 pts

Conv → Meeting %
20%
20 pts


Everything is based of the leader in the category. For example Mallory had 6 meeting so she won 35 points on that BUT Hannah had 11.7 points for the 2 meetings she had. This is how she earned points there, 

(Hannah's meetings ÷ Top meetings) × Max points

(2 ÷ 6) × 35 = 0.333 × 35 = 11.67 → rounded to 11.7.


I also added which team won based on the stats their team members produced for the week.



This is just the starting point, there is so much we can do with it.

body:
Carrier Note, Great American (Level Funded Program)

Thank you to @Lior Goldstein  for sending over the information on Great American. The products look interesting and we will be testing them with a few groups to see how they perform.

I just got off the phone with Kristen Comier, their Sales Director, and had a very productive and good conversation.

A few notable items:

• Their lead underwriter previously helped build the Allstate Health underwriting/app system, so their processes and structure are very similar to how Allstate operates.

• Kristen knows Gabe and Justin from Allstate, and actually trained Justin when he first became a VP there, so they are very familiar with the way our block was written.

• Much of their health program team is former Allstate personnel, and the program is built in a very similar way operationally.

Program Highlights

• Paper: A-rated paper

• Markets: Available in all states except Hawaii and Washington

• Network options: Cigna or RBP / RBP wrap structures

Underwriting Structure

• Groups under 10: Will write with IMQs

• 10+ employees: AI underwriting, no IMQs required



Important Underwriting Note

Because their underwriting team already knows our book from Allstate and understands how our groups typically perform, they have removed the normal participation requirements for our agency, meaning we do not need to meet the typical 50% participation threshold when submitting groups.

Additional Notes

They also offer P&C products, which I will review with Dazos to see if there is any opportunity there.

For now, the plan is to run a few test groups through them and evaluate results. Once we have some quotes and early experience, I will circulate an update.

@Declan McGettigan  – Please send them our standard carrier questionnaire for completion.

Contact

Kristen Comier

Sales Director

Phone: 508-868-3798

Email: kcomier@gaig.com

body:
RFP Tool – Requested Updates / Clarifications

1. Follow-Up Tool Logic & Behavior
•	Cadence-Based Visibility
•	Items should only appear on the follow-up board based on follow-up timing rules.
•	Example:
•	If a carrier (e.g., Nationwide) has not returned a quote within 2 days, it should automatically appear as:
•	“Not Received – Needs Follow-Up”
•	The board should function like a “zero inbox” system:
•	Only items requiring action appear
•	Clean, minimal, and actionable at all times
•	Reduce Clutter (Groups Cleanup)
•	Current state (~170 groups) is too cluttered and confusing
•	Request:
•	Reset/clear out all legacy groups created before the follow-up tool was implemented
•	Start fresh from roughly “last week” (tool creation point)
•	Target: ~10–20 clean, relevant groups
•	Testing Environment
•	Cleaner dataset will allow:
•	Easier testing of the feature
•	Better training/practice with VA
•	More controlled rollout of workflow

⸻

2. Side-by-Side Comparison Enhancements
•	Custom Plan Selection (Core Requirement)
•	Ability to manually select and compare specific plans within quotes
•	Example:
•	If a quote has 9 plans:
•	Select Plan 1 and Plan 3 only
•	Compare those specifically
•	Should support:
•	Selecting plans across different quotes
•	Flexible pairing (not locked to full-quote comparisons)
•	Drag-and-Drop / Selection UI
•	Ideal interaction:
•	Drag-and-drop plans into comparison
•	Or checkbox-style selection from proposals/SBCs
•	Goal: Make comparison building intuitive and fast
•	Separate Comparison Types (Medical vs Ancillary)
•	Ability to run different comparison workflows depending on product type
•	Medical
•	Standard side-by-side comparison (existing concept)
•	Ancillary (e.g., Beam, Principal)
•	Separate comparison flow/interface
•	Avoid mixing with medical comparisons
•	Should not allow irrelevant comparisons (e.g., mismatched product types)

author: Samuel Ghirmay
body:
@Aharon Heiman @Jack Harris Good morning guys, sorry for the delay on this one, it is a bit weird. I have made plenty substantive changes. The case law is not really important(it can be omitted) but the principle is still the same. I understand this is not one we will directly sign but the clients .. correct? If that is the case I may have done a bit much but you guys can decide what is important. I will also be working on it a bit more, I just want to share what I have done so far.



Here are the things I changed ;



Third-Party Services
Added that Provider remains fully responsible for third-party suppliers, and that Client must comply with third-party terms only if provided in writing and not conflicting with the Agreement.

Client Data and Responsibilities
Deleted text suggesting Client is solely responsible for data accuracy and adequacy, and revised to specify that Provider remains responsible for any data it modifies or transmits on Client's behalf; clarified that nothing limits Provider's obligations/liabilities and removed prior language disclaiming Provider's engagement in the practice of medicine or its liability for acts/failures relating thereto.

Disputed Amounts
Extended Client's notification period for invoice disputes from five business days to thirty days, and clarified that failure to dispute on time does not waive Client’s right to later dispute undiscovered charges.

Services; Intellectual Property
Deleted automatic assignment of feedback/IP from Client to Provider, and replaced with a nonexclusive, perpetual, royalty-free license for Provider to use feedback (excluding Client Confidential Information) to improve services, without assigning underlying Client IP rights; specified that Client retains rights in Client data and Provider’s license to use is limited to performance of services.

Disclaimer
Replaced broad covenants not to assert claims with a clause that clarifies the Client’s rights and remedies are not limited for Provider's breach, negligence, willful misconduct, or failure to perform under the Agreement. Also clarified that Provider’s products are not its own but instead insurance products of third-parties.

Indemnification
Limited Client’s indemnification obligations to actions or breaches solely attributable to Client (rather than broader liability for affiliates and insurance carriers); revised language to clarify scope and basis of each party’s indemnification, including clarifying trigger events and deleting references to claims against Provider by any party except as attributable to Client’s actions.

Limitation of Liability
Replaced Provider’s specific limitation with a mutual limitation on aggregate liability for all parties, extending the look-back period for damages from six to twelve months prior to the claim, and clarified distinctions between amounts paid and payable.

Affiliates
Removed Provider’s discretionary right to require separate service agreements with Client affiliates and clarified that Client is not liable for any obligations of an affiliate unless expressly so agreed in writing.

Term and Termination
Replaced an at-will termination clause with a term-based structure: initial one-year term, auto-renewal for one-year periods, with termination rights for convenience (30 days’ prior notice) or for cause (on 10 days’ notice of uncured breach).

Non-Competition
Shortened the post-termination non-compete from two years to six months and narrowed the Restricted Area from the United States to only counties where Provider marketed or provided services in the last 12 months.

Non-Solicitation
Clarified non-solicitation to prohibit only solicitation for competing services and only with clients with whom Client had material contact in the last 12 months, providing exceptions for general advertisements and responses to unsolicited inquiries.

Publicity/Reference Rights
Revised Provider’s rights to use Client’s name/trademarks and disclose the nature of services, making these subj

author: Aharon Heiman
body:
@Samuel Ghirmay Ill call and explain



Thanks for sending over the agreement. We’re aligned on moving forward.

We’ve reviewed internally and made a few adjustments to bring the agreement in line with standard commercial terms, particularly around liability, post-termination obligations, and competitive restrictions.

None of these are intended to change the business relationship, just to ensure balanced risk allocation for both parties.

1. Liability Cap
Replace with: Provider liability capped at 12 months of fees or agreed minimum.
2. Provider Disclaimer
Add obligation to comply with applicable laws and industry standards.
3. Indemnification
Make mutual and limited to each party’s negligence, breach, or misconduct.
4. Non-Compete
Remove or reduce to 6–12 month non-solicitation only.
5. Termination Fees
Remove obligation to pay remaining contract term after termination.
