Hi @Aharon Heiman,



Sorry for the delay I thought I sent this one off already. Here are the changes made as requested.



Third-Party Services Added that any third-party service terms must not diminish the Provider’s obligations under the agreement or applicable law, and must include compliance with industry standards.

Provider Obligations Added explicit requirement for Provider to comply with all applicable laws and industry standards. Required Provider to implement safeguards for protected health information and notify Client of any security incidents or breaches within ten business days.

Payment; ACH & Collection Substantially revised ACH/automatic withdrawal provisions: deleted Provider's ability to set up automatic drafts without Client’s written authorization and prohibited automatic debits for disputed amounts; Provider may now only pursue collection consistent with law, and may not unilaterally charge Client accounts or credit cards. Clarified that ACH authorizations must be provided by Client for each transaction, not on a standing basis. Language regarding maintenance of sufficient funds and unilateral Provider withdrawal authority was deleted and replaced with more Client-protective provisions.

Losses Added clarification that Client is only responsible for direct losses relating to its own errors or omissions and Provider cannot recover for indirect, incidental, special, exemplary, punitive, or consequential damages from the Client’s conduct.

Disclaimer Added that the Provider’s disclaimer does not limit its obligation to comply with applicable law; clarified Client's waiver of claims, but now with an exception for Provider negligence, willful misconduct, or breach.

Indemnification Revised from unilateral (Client-to-Provider) indemnity to mutual, with each party indemnifying the other for losses arising from its own negligence, willful misconduct, or breach, or third-party claims for intellectual property infringement. Clarified that gross negligence standard was removed and replaced with “negligence.” Deleted coverage of lawsuits unrelated to the agreement and clarified the scope of indemnified parties.

Limitation of Liability Changed cap on liability from six months to twelve months’ fees paid or a mutually agreed minimum cap, whichever is greater; changed “Provider nor Provider’s licensors” to “Party” to reflect symmetry; other minor clarifications.

Term & Termination Revised to permit either party to terminate the agreement by written notice to the other at any time, with no early termination fee except for fees accrued through the effective date.

Non-Solicitation/Non-Compete (now titled "Non-Solicitation") Substantively narrowed restrictions: deleted broad non-compete language and focus is now only on non-solicitation/diversion of Provider’s current customers, prospective clients, and current employees for twelve months post-termination, with general solicitations expressly carved out as non-violative.

@Aharon Heiman @Jack Harris @Lior Goldstein  Hello everyone, I hope your weekend is going well. Sorry for the late-night update. Here is the redlined version of the contract. I took a bit more time on it since you mentioned it is one of the biggest contracts affecting us. These are the changes I made; if you notice anything or if I missed something, please let me know, and I will address it.



Transition to Nationwide Life and Benefits Insurance Company Added that any future changes materially and adversely affecting the Agency’s rights or compensation require the Agency’s prior written consent.

Indemnification – Non-Company Products Clarified the Agency’s indemnification duty to cover “third-party” claims and modified the trigger for such indemnity to cover “gross negligence or willful misconduct in connection with” non-Company Products. Removed coverage for any related claims and replaced broad causation language with more specific connection language.

Appointment and Termination of Representatives Added that Company’s right to terminate appointments must be for reasonable cause and after written notice, removing the Company’s sole discretion; clarified process for appointment and removal of Agents and added detail on Agency’s insurance obligations, limiting the Agency’s obligation to obtaining reasonable assurances rather than guaranteeing insurance compliance.

Compensation; Company Lien Limited Company’s lien on compensation to undisputed amounts payable under the Agreement only, introduced a requirement for written notice and the opportunity to dispute before offsetting, and excluded debts not relating to this Agreement from setoff.

Termination Provisions Added that Agency retains the right to all earned compensation upon termination without cause; narrowed the grounds for immediate termination based on accounting irregularities or regulatory investigation to instances “materially” affecting Company’s interests, and required good faith determinations. Changed “reckless” to “willful misconduct” and limited determinations to a court of competent jurisdiction or based on reasonable evidence. Revised change of control termination rights to require a material adverse effect and good faith consultation. Upon termination for cause, clarified Agency’s right to accrued and vested compensation.

Agency Indemnification of Company Rewrote indemnification language to clarify that Agency indemnifies Company only for third-party claims arising from Agency’s gross negligence, willful misconduct, unauthorized warranties, or material breach, subject to prompt notice, defense rights, and excluding claims caused by Company or its affiliates.

Delegation Explicitly added that Company remains fully responsible for the performance of any obligations delegated to administrative agents.

Confidential Records and Audit Specified that records must be delivered only upon reasonable prior written request as required by law or for compliance verification, and allowed Agency to retain copies for legal, regulatory, or archival purposes.

Amendments and Modifications Changed amendment mechanism so that both Parties must execute changes in writing, prohibited unilateral amendment except as required by law, specified minimum notice and effect of legally-required amendments, and clarified that compensation changes may only occur by mutual written agreement or to the minimum necessary extent required by law. Removed Company’s unilateral discretion to modify compensation.

General Provisions General tightening throughout to ensure determinations are made in good faith, based on reasonable evidence, or by mutual agreement, with several instances of prior broad discretion narrowed or removed.



Best regards,

Samuel Ghirmay.

author: Aharon Heiman
body:
Tax Advice Disclaimer — Limit, Don't Eliminate
----------------------------------------------

The current language ("We do not provide legal, financial, or tax advice") is too broad. Innovative BPS has a separate line of business that does provide tax advisory services. Please revise to carve that out — something like "except as expressly provided through Innovative BPS's tax advisory services."

Keep a general disclaimer covering casual/conversational guidance (e.g., flagging to a client that S-corp owners should verify deduction eligibility with their CPA). That kind of heads-up should remain covered by disclaimer language — we are not giving formal tax advice in those moments, but we want protection against it being construed that way.

Fiduciary Language — Limit, Don't Eliminate
-------------------------------------------

The current draft positions Innovative BPS as not a fiduciary. That's inaccurate — we are a fiduciary to the extent a broker is a fiduciary. Please rework to limit the scope of fiduciary duty (e.g., bounded to the broker-of-record role and the specific services in this agreement) rather than disclaim it entirely.

Carrier Communication — Rewrite
-------------------------------

The draft language suggesting Innovative BPS does not act as the intermediary between client and carrier is inaccurate — that is literally the core broker function. Two acceptable paths:

* Eliminate that disclaimer entirely, or
* Qualify it: "except in the regular course of services as broker of record" (or similar)

Scope of Disclaimers — Narrow Them
----------------------------------

Several disclaimers in the current draft are written more broadly than the deductions service requires. Please narrow them so they apply only to the specific service at issue, not as blanket carve-outs across all of Innovative BPS's services.

* $10 PEPM GA override to start; revisit and bump once production hits ~$3M in 6 months
* Separate GA quoting portal, clean data separation for downline agents
* "Innovative GA" as the working name; can rename as Pinecrest/Innovative brands consolidate
* Light lift: E&O + agency licenses for GA and downlines, no major red tape
* Strictly level-funded; no fully insured
Next steps: Arlo builds portal (EOW) · finalize GA name with Arlo · follow up with interested broker

body:
Great American's response on the GA:

They are not appointing any new GAs at this time — they only have a couple in the country and are still figuring out their GA philosophy and what it will look like going forward.

They noted their existing GAs are smaller, regionally based (Midwest), and level-funded only, with John Walker in Texas serving as a successful test case. Their GA contracts are one-year terms with on-the-books volume requirements, reevaluated annually.

They were clear they weren't saying no outright — just talking it through — and preferred to start with their current model and see where it leads. They agreed a broker split was workable in the meantime (just requires both parties be appointed). Ultimately they wanted to revisit the GA discussion in six months to a year as business grows, and asked to keep communication open.

@Jack Harris 

----------------------------------
CoAdvantage PEO Intro Call — Recap
----------------------------------

Productive intro call with Austin O'Brien (CoAdvantage), brought in by Chase to fill our PEO/ASO gap as we stand up the GA. CoAdvantage is the largest privately held PEO, has proprietary tech, and merged with PrimePay last year — so they cover everything from shell payroll to full co-employment. Chase vouched that they're the only PEO he'd refer benefits to.

DSP angle: still can't go full PEO for medical, but ASO (HR + workers' comp + payroll, client keeps FEIN) and pay-as-you-go workers' comp are real openings. Austin's checking internally on DSP workers' comp and EPLI appetite.

Economics: commissions start at 20% of admin fee with a ~$1,000/head target, scaling to 40% on volume.

The bigger play is the GA side, where non-DSP brokers need PEO options we don't currently have. Austin also flagged co-BOR arrangements (we keep Ease, they handle admin) for groups ~150+.

Friction point to flag: Judah may not love this one — Austin mentioned proprietary tech, but worth pressure-testing whether that's truly in-house or white-labeled PrismHR underneath. Most PEOs run on PrismHR and the customer service reputation isn't great. Need to confirm before we commit.

Next steps:

* Julianne Randolph (channel partner liaison) intro for onboarding and spiffs
* Austin sending RFP requirements email and average payroll pricing
* Internal checks on DSP workers' comp and EPLI appetite
* On our end: decide where ASO + pay-as-you-go workers' comp fits in our DSP offering

author: Samuel Ghirmay
body:
@Jack Harris @Aharon Heiman  Ok, here is the redline for LHH. There is a potential (hopefully minor) issue with section 3.2 and section 2 of Exhibit A would like to discuss with you.

Representative Coverage / Responsibility Revised Referring Broker’s responsibility language to clarify responsibility for Representatives only within written authorization and not to the extent caused by LLH instructions/materials/omissions/approvals.

Expenses Deleted the blanket rule that all Referring Broker expenses are borne exclusively by Referring Broker and added that LLH must bear such expenses where it has approved them in writing in advance or where expenses arise from LLH’s breach.

Referral Referring Broker; LLH Election; Compensation Timing Deleted LLH’s prior “sole and unfettered discretion” option to enter/decline agreements and replaced it with a good-faith, legitimate-business/legal/underwriting/compliance/credit rationale framework, and revised compensation to add that LLH owes no Referral Fees if it declines (including clarification that LLH owes Referring Broker unless LLH later enters into an agreement within twelve months after a documented referral).

LLH Modifications / Enrollment of Substantially Similar Programs Replaced “efficient/expedient/desirable” and “sole discretion/without prior notice” concepts with a “reasonably necessary for business” standard and added enumerated legal/compliance/admin reasons, while expanding LLH’s right to enroll Clients in substantially similar third-party-controlled plans only if LLH provides prior written notice and such transfers do not reduce Referral Fees.

Termination for Cause Standard (Referring Broker) Deleted the prior trigger phrasing allowing compensation to continue through a defined period and revised the termination-for-cause consequence so Referring Broker compensation only continues through the end of the current coverage period for the affected Client, with no longer association effective with the next succeeding payroll period.

Client Appointments / Non-Exclusivity; Geographic and Fee Protection Added an express limitation preventing LLH from using Referring Broker’s Confidential Information or documented referral pipeline to avoid Referral Fees, while retaining LLH’s right to appoint other brokers and directly market/solicit in the same territories.

Materials; Ownership Added that all pre-existing Referring Broker materials, know-how, client relationships, and IP remain Referring Broker’s exclusive property, and did not change the prohibition against altering or using insurance/LLH-provided Materials without required approvals.

LLH’s Contract/Enrollment Control; Fee Non-Reduction Added a prospective-only modification restriction and a non-reduction protection so amendments apply prospectively only and do not reduce Referral Fees earned or payable for Clients referred before the effective date.

Payment Timing / Administrative Actions (Fee Reduction) Incorporated an administrative-fee reduction mechanism (with prior written approval) that reduces monthly Referral Fee by half the difference between $40.00 and the actual administrative fee, while preserving a $5.00 minimum PEPM Referral Fee.

Effective Date / Exhibit Control Revised conflict/discrepancy hierarchy to add that the Exhibit controls with respect to Product(s), Fees, Referral Fees, payment timing, and other compensation terms, and that the Agreement controls in all other respects.

Indemnity Scope (Referring Broker) Deleted an “any and all” framing and broadened specificity by changing the covered claims and recasting the indemnity to apply “to the extent arising” from Referring Broker’s gross negligence/willful misconduct/violation of law/material breach (instead of earlier “error/omission” and “any wrongful act” language), while adding an express carve-out for costs/attorneys’ fees except to the extent caused by an Indemnified Person’s negligence/willful misconduct/violation of law/breach.

Entire Agreeme

body:
ITC Vegas (InsureTech Connect) 2026

When/Where: September 29 – October 1, 2026 | Mandalay Bay, Las Vegas Insurtechuk

What it is: The world's largest and most influential insurance innovation conference, designed to go beyond traditional conferences — where bold ideas meet real-world action across underwriting, claims, distribution, customer experience, and AI. Paktolus

Scale: Over 9,000 industry participants from across the globe, including insurance executives, insurtech founders, VCs, brokers, agents, and risk managers. More than 500 of the most influential minds in insurance, technology, and innovation take the stage. insurtechexpressITC Vegas 2026

Primary themes: The 2026 theme is The Horizon of Possibilities: Predict, Prepare, Progress — foresight-to-action across distribution tech, AI, and industry transformation.

Relevance to Pinecrest/Innovative: Higher than Reuters for a brokerage/BPS operation. The audience mix includes distribution-side players (brokers, agents, carriers, insurtechs, investors), which is more aligned with where Innovative intersects the market. Brokers and agents are explicitly part of the attendee profile. The sheer scale creates outsized networking density — more DSP-adjacent HR tech vendors, benefits platforms, and carrier reps than anywhere else under one roof. insurtechexpress

Attendance necessity: Worth 1–2 people if your intent is vendor intelligence, carrier relationship-building, or scoping emerging tech (benefits admin platforms, ACA tools, Section 125 solutions). Don't send more than that unless you're sponsoring or presenting. @Eli Tabaria @Jack Harris @Aharon Heiman

author: Declan McGettigan
body:
Reuters Future of Insurance USA 2026

When/Where: June 25–26, 2026 | Marriott Marquis Chicago Reutersevents

What it is: An exclusive community of 500+ technology, product, customer, and risk leaders focused on fusing competing strategic tensions into one integrated approach that doesn't compromise on customer centricity, digital literacy, or long-term growth. C-suite speakers from Markel, MetLife, AXA XL, Liberty Mutual, and similar carriers headline. insurtechexpress

Scale: ~500 attendees — intentionally smaller, more curated than ITC.

Primary themes: AI-powered underwriting and claims, legacy modernization, agentic AI, customer journey personalization.

Relevance to Pinecrest/Innovative: Moderate-to-low as an attendee; higher as a sponsor or thought-leadership play. Carriers are actively seeking solutions at in-person channels, and the event facilitates direct access to decision-makers in senior leadership positions. The audience is mostly carrier-side tech and ops leaders — useful for intelligence on where your carrier partners are heading (AI in underwriting, benefits administration changes), but this isn't a broker/DSP-facing event. If Innovative is building out carrier relationships or positioning services to carriers, the smaller format means higher-quality access. Reutersevents

Attendance necessity: Low for general staff; potentially worthwhile for 1 senior person tracking carrier strategy and product roadmap. @Aharon Heiman @Jack Harris
